main_ingredients: mixed vegetables (capsicum, tomato, onion, cauliflower, peas, carrot)
kadhai masala (coriander, cumin, dried red chilli, garam masala)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Veg Kadhai is approximately 300 g, served in or alongside a kadhai (wok), usually accompanied by naan, roti, or rice.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Mixed Vegetable Curry / Vegetable Masala Dish
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN does not list 'Veg Kadhai' by that exact name. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN data for mixed vegetable curry / vegetable masala preparations with comparable ingredient profiles (mixed vegetables, tomato-onion gravy, oil/ghee, whole spices). Energy and macros are consistent with USDA FoodData Central reference data for similar Indian vegetable curry dishes. Adaptation is documented; confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — mixed vegetable curry / vegetable-based Indian dish analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Veg Kadhai in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on mixed vegetable curry entries in the Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., 2021, Diabetes Care). Mixed vegetable curries with tomato-onion gravy and minimal starchy carbohydrate consistently fall in the low GI range (GI 40–50). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 31.5) / 100 = 14.2.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg per 100 g; ~930 mg per restaurant serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that a full restaurant serving of Veg Kadhai can contribute approximately 930 mg sodium (~40% of the 2300 mg daily limit), and should request reduced-salt preparation or limit portion size accordingly.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~45) and high dietary fibre from mixed vegetables
type2_diabetes.guidance: Veg Kadhai has a low estimated GI of approximately 45 and provides dietary fibre (~2.4 g per 100 g) from mixed vegetables, supporting moderate postprandial glucose response; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes can include it as part of a balanced meal when portion size and accompanying carbohydrate (rice, bread) are controlled.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate fat content with predominantly unsaturated fats; rich in vegetables providing antioxidants and fibre
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Veg Kadhai prepared with minimal ghee or oil provides a favourable unsaturated-to-saturated fat ratio and vegetable-derived antioxidants (vitamin C, vitamin A, polyphenols), making it a heart-friendly choice when sodium and cooking fat are kept moderate.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Veg Kadhai (also spelled Veg Kadai or Kadhai Sabzi) is a popular North Indian vegetable dish cooked in a kadhai — a thick, circular, deep cooking vessel resembling a wok. The dish is characterised by a semi-dry, intensely spiced tomato-onion masala base in which mixed vegetables such as capsicum (bell pepper), tomato, cauliflower, green peas, carrot, and onion are wok-tossed at high heat. The cooking technique imparts a slight char and smoky depth to the vegetables. The spice blend, known as kadhai masala, typically includes freshly ground coriander seeds, cumin, dried red chillies, and garam masala, giving the dish a bold, aromatic, and mildly pungent flavour profile. Ghee or neutral oil is used for tempering whole spices before the vegetables and tomato-onion gravy are added. The texture is chunky with tender-crisp vegetables coated in a thick, clinging sauce. Veg Kadhai is a staple on Indian restaurant menus across India, Singapore, and the wider South Asian diaspora. It is served with naan, tandoori roti, paratha, or steamed basmati rice. Nutritionally, it is a moderate-energy dish (~98 kcal per 100 g) with a good dietary fibre content from the mixed vegetables, a low estimated glycemic index (~45), and meaningful micronutrient contributions including vitamin C, vitamin A, iron, and folate. The sodium content is moderate to high in restaurant preparations and warrants attention for individuals managing hypertension. It is naturally vegetarian, gluten-free, and halal-compatible.

confidence_reason: ICMR-NIN does not list Veg Kadhai by exact name; nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN mixed vegetable curry / vegetable masala data with cross-reference to USDA FoodData Central for similar preparations. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for mixed vegetable curry dishes. Confidence is medium due to adaptation rather than exact source match.
notes: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN (Nutritive Value of Indian Foods) data for mixed vegetable curry and vegetable masala preparations, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy) entries for comparable Indian vegetable curry dishes. No exact 'Veg Kadhai' entry was found in any approved primary source; adaptation is documented. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in ICMR-NIN or USDA data for this food category. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this dish type. Trans fat is 0.0 — confirmed negligible/trace in vegetable-based Indian curry preparations per ICMR-NIN; this is a confirmed zero for a plant-based dish with no hydrogenated fat. Vitamin B12 is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this is a fully plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients contributing B12 (ghee contributes negligible B12 at typical use levels, confirmed trace per USDA). GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on mixed vegetable curry entries in the University of Sydney GI Database; glycemic load per serving = (45 × 31.5 g carbohydrate per 300 g serving) / 100 = 14.2. This record was generated from a field survey data seed record (GD003300) served by Dugout - Circular Road. The cuisine_tag 'Butter Chicken' in the input appears to be a platform tagging artefact and has not been carried forward as it does not describe this dish.
